coredata.dc:title: Ultrafast detection of folic acid in nanomolar levels and cancer cell imaging using hydrothermally synthesized carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Krishnapriya, T.K.
Prasanth, S.
Deepti, Ayswaria
Baby Chakrapani, P.S.
Asha, A.S.
Jayaraj, M.K.
coredata.dc:description: The development of an ultrafast folic acid (FA) sensor (with no incubation time), having a detection limit and detection range in the nanomolar levels is of high importance in the field of biomedical FA sensing. For that, highly luminescent carbon dots (CDs) are synthesized by hydrothermal method with a lower growth duration of ‘1 h’ using the precursors, anhydrous citric acid as the carbon source and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen dopant material. TEM images confirmed a spherical morphology for the prepared CDs with an average size of nearly 2.8 nm. A photoluminescence quenching-based technique is used for FA detection using the prepared CDs. The detection limit is 6 nM, the lowest reported for CDs made from these precursors and the detection range is 0–50 nM. The mode of quenching is found to be static, based on the lifetime measurements and UV–vis spectroscopy. The mechanism of static quenching due to the formation of a ground state complex between the synthesized CDs and FA is confirmed by Raman spectroscopy and dynamic light scattering (DLS) study, and the binding mechanism of CDs and FA is explained in detail using FTIR and Raman spectroscopy for the first time. The fabricated CDs are used as biocompatible probes to image the N2a cells. A quantitative reduction in fluorescence intensity was observed for CD + FA mixtures internalized into the cells with incremental addition of FA. Furthermore, the practicality of the CD probe as an FA sensor is evaluated by testing the amount of FA in spiked plasma samples from albino rats, and the results are validated using a clinical method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cell imaging
Folic acid sensor
Static quenching
Ground-state complex

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Biocompatibility studies and cell imaging using CDs
Biocompatibility and cell imaging using CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Biotechnology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Kerala State Council for Science, Technology and Environment

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by Kerala State Council for Science, Technology and Environment (KSCSTE) under the KSCSTE Research fellowship scheme (KSCSTE/687/2019 -FSHP -PS).

This work was funded by Kerala State Council for Science, Technology and Environment (KSCSTE) under the KSCSTE Research fellowship scheme (KSCSTE/687/2019-FSHP-PS). The authors acknowledge DST FIST for the FESEM facility at the Department of Physics, CUSAT, STIC-CUSAT for TEM measurement, the International School of Photonics, CUSAT for DLS measurement, and Dr. Saritha G Bhatt, Department of Biotechnology, CUSAT for fruitful discussions.

This work was funded by Kerala State Council for Science, Technology and Environment (KSCSTE) under the KSCSTE Research fellowship scheme (KSCSTE/687/2019-FSHP-PS). The authors acknowledge DST FIST for the FESEM facility at the Department of Physics, CUSAT, STIC-CUSAT for TEM measurement, the International School of Photonics, CUSAT for DLS measurement, and Dr. Saritha G Bhatt, Department of Biotechnology, CUSAT for fruitful discussions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis of CDs and folic acid sensing using the prepared CDs. Above: (a) Hydrothermal synthesis of CDs using CA and EDA as precursors, (b) dialysis of the prepared solution, (c) obtained CDs and (d) transparent solution of CDs in visible light and blue luminescence under UV light. Below: Mechanism of FA sensing. The quenching of luminescence by complex formation of CDs and FA is represented.

(a) TEM image of CD1 showing the spherical nanoparticles (b) excitation dependent emission spectra of CD1 (Inset clearly shows the shift in PL peak to higher wavelengths as the excitation peak increases from 370 nm to 430 nm) (c) MTT biocompatibility assay of CD1 and CD6 in N2a cell line showing good cell viability. The error bar represents the standard deviation between five biological replicates.

Fluorescent microscope bright field images of N2a cells (a) untreated (c) treated with CD1 and (e) treated with CD6, (b), (d) and (f) blue filter images of untreated cells, treated with CD1 and CD6 respectively (The scale bar is 50 μm in all the images).

(a) Stern-Volmer (SV) plot of FA sensing using CDs having linear behavior in the nM range of FA. Five parallel experiments were conducted, and the average is plotted with error bars for the detection of FA (b) Absorbance spectra of FA along with excitation and emission spectra of CDs showing the innerfilter effect.

(a) UV– Vis absorption spectra of CDs, CD + FA mixture and FA. DLS Spectrum of (b) CDs (c) CD + FA 50 nM indicating the formation of a complex between CDs and FA with larger hydrodynamic diameter with an increase in FA concentration (d) Raman spectra of CDs, FA and CD + FA mixture (e) FTIR spectra of CDs and CD + FA mixture showing a shift in CN and NN vibrational peaks.
Lifetime measurement of CDs and CD + FA (different concentrations).
Analytical performance of the proposed method in the determination of FA in the blood plasma of albino mouse.

head.ce:title: Ultrafast detection of folic acid in nanomolar levels and cancer cell imaging using hydrothermally synthesized carbon dots

Cochin University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of an ultrafast folic acid (FA) sensor (with no incubation time), having a detection limit and detection range in the nanomolar levels is of high importance in the field of biomedical FA sensing. For that, highly luminescent carbon dots (CDs) are synthesized by hydrothermal method with a lower growth duration of ‘1 h’ using the precursors, anhydrous citric acid as the carbon source and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen dopant material. TEM images confirmed a spherical morphology for the prepared CDs with an average size of nearly 2.8 nm. A photoluminescence quenching-based technique is used for FA detection using the prepared CDs. The detection limit is 6 nM, the lowest reported for CDs made from these precursors and the detection range is 0–50 nM. The mode of quenching is found to be static, based on the lifetime measurements and UV–vis spectroscopy. The mechanism of static quenching due to the formation of a ground state complex between the synthesized CDs and FA is confirmed by Raman spectroscopy and dynamic light scattering (DLS) study, and the binding mechanism of CDs and FA is explained in detail using FTIR and Raman spectroscopy for the first time. The fabricated CDs are used as biocompatible probes to image the N2a cells. A quantitative reduction in fluorescence intensity was observed for CD + FA mixtures internalized into the cells with incremental addition of FA. Furthermore, the practicality of the CD probe as an FA sensor is evaluated by testing the amount of FA in spiked plasma samples from albino rats, and the results are validated using a clinical method.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), zero-dimensional nanostructures of carbon, have gained wide attention since their accidental discovery in 2004 [1]. CDs have a graphitic core that is responsible for their crystalline nature and several surface groups which determine their luminescent properties [2–4]. A variety of applications, including optoelectronics, photovoltaics, photocatalysis, bioimaging and biosensing exploit the optical properties of CDs’. Exceptional properties of CDs such as highly intense excitation dependent photoluminescence [5], good biocompatibility [6,7], photostability [8], water solubility [9], high quantum yield (QY) [10] and low toxicity [11] compared to other conventional quantum dots make it a good candidate for bio-applications [9,12]. The optical properties of CDs depend on various factors such as domains of conjugated sp2 carbon, photophysical processes in the core, edge and surface states, doping with different atoms and the presence of molecular fluorophores [13–15]. Enhancement of the QY is possible by doping CDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen [16], sulfur [17] and phosphorous [18,19]. Among the various top-down and bottom-up approaches for the synthesis of CDs, the hydrothermal method stands out as a simple, low-cost, and facile technique [20]. Citric acid (CA) is a commonly chosen carbon source for the synthesis of CDs owing to its unique chemical structure with a variety of reactive functional groups attached to it. Surface passivation of CDs by nitrogen dopant materials such as ethylenediamine (EDA), triethylene pentaamine (TEPA) and hexamethylene tetramine has been widely studied to enhance their luminescence properties [8,18]. Conventional CDs have a hydrophobic carbon core and hydrophilic functional groups on their surface [3].

Folic acid (FA), a member of water-soluble B- group vitamins is derived from folate polyglutamates [21,22]. FA has a major role in our body in helping the maturation of young cells in bone marrow [23]. Both the deficiency and excess of FA in the human body have adverse effects. The deficiency of FA can result in birth defects and congenital heart defects [24]. FA is very important in the first trimester for pregnant women for the healthy growth of the fetus and the lack of FA is connected with Alzheimer’s disease, cardiovascular disease and mental illnesses [23]. Excess FA leads to vomiting, nausea, zinc deficiency, intestinal problems etc. Hence, the proper detection of FA in the human body is of utmost importance. A variety of techniques such as surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), electrochemical assays, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) etc. have been used for the quantitative determination of FA. The requirement for expensive instrumental setup, professional operators and prolonged time consumption are some of the limitations of these methods [25].

Fluorescence based detection of FA stands out as a better option because of its high sensitivity, quick response, and simplicity in sample preparation [21,23,26]. Recently, Zhang et al. [26] reported the use of CDs prepared using CA and ethylene imine by pyrolysis method for FA detection. One of the difficulties in this study was the long incubation time of 9 h for effective detection, and the limit of detection (LoD) was in the micromolar (μM) range. Li et al. [25] proposed a paper based FA detection using CDs prepared using CA and EDA. But again, the LoD was in the μM range and the duration of hydrothermal synthesis of CDs was long. A comparison of reported works on FA sensing using CDs is given in Table 1S.

Lengthy growth time for CDs during hydrothermal synthesis and long incubation time for CDs + FA mixture during FA sensing are the two main drawbacks of CDs based FA sensing reported till date (Tables 1S & 2S) [25,26]. Linearity in response and a limit of detection in the nM range is highly preferable since the normal range of FA in the human blood serum is 4.5– 34 nM or 2–15 ng/ml [27]. In this study, blue luminescent nitrogen doped CDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method in a comparatively low growth time of 1 h. The biocompatible CDs thus prepared were utilized for in vitro cell imaging. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on ultrafast detection of the nanomolar (nM) range of FA with a linear response in the nM range, using CDs prepared by using the precursors, CA and EDA. The linear behavior of detection in the nM levels of FA shows its tremendous potential in the medical field and the detection of FA in plasma samples of albino mice proves its practicality in real samples. The paper also provides an explanation of the mechanism of FA sensing considering the optical, Raman, lifetime and DLS studies of the prepared CDs.

Citric acid anhydrous (CA) and 1,2- ethylenediamine (EDA) was of analytical reagent grade and was purchased from Merck. FA was of Alfa Aesar. Histidine (His), tryptophan (Try), tyrosine (Tyr), vitamin C, glucose and catechol were of Sigma Aldrich. Invitrogen MTT (3- (4, 5-Dimethylthiazol-2–41)-2,5- diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) CAT # M6494 was of Sigma Aldrich. All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

Carbon dots were synthesized using CA and EDA, where CA is the raw precursor of carbon and EDA is the nitrogen doping reagent. Hydrothermal carbonization reaction of three acid groups of CA with two amine groups of EDA results in highly fluorescent CDs. A schematic representation of the synthesis procedure adapted from Wang et al. [8] is shown in (Fig. 1 ).

CA (2 g) and EDA (1.4 mL) were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water. The solution was heated at 200 °C in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for different time durations; 1 h, 3 h, 5 h and 6 h. The prepared samples were named as CD1, CD3, CD5 and CD6 respectively. CDs prepared at 200 °C, are reported to give better fluorescence properties due to the easy passivation and carbonization occurring at this temperature [16]. The resultant solution, which was dark brown in color, was neutralized with NaOH solution and dialyzed for 24 h using a dialysis membrane (MWCO 2 KDa from Sigma Aldrich) to eliminate the additional salts and reagents.

A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM), Jeol/ JEM 2100, having an acceleration voltage of 200 kV has been utilized to examine the CDs' morphology, size and interplanar spacing. The crystallinity of the prepared CDs was determined using the Rigaku MiniFlex X-ray diffractometer with 1.5418 Å, CuKα radiation as the X-ray source. HORIBA PTI QM-8075–11-C spectrofluorometer was used to investigate the photoluminescence properties (both excitation and emission), QY, and lifetime of the CDs and CD + FA samples, with a 150 W Xenon lamp as the excitation source. QY was measured using an integrating sphere attached to the spectrofluorometer. PL spectra of the CDs solution and the reference solution (water) at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm was taken in the full range 350–650 nm (including excitation and emission range). Difference in the integral intensity of emission and excitation of both the sample and reference solutions are calculated using the software application ‘quantum yield calculator’ to get the value of QY. UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was done in the wavelength range of 200 nm to 850 nm with a JASCO V-570 spectrophotometer. Attenuated total reflection infrared (ATR-IR) spectra were taken for the lyophilized CDs samples using Thermo Nicolet iS50 spectrometer and FTIR spectra of CDs and CD + FA mixture were taken using Jasco FT/IR-4100 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. Micro-Raman spectrometer (HORIBA JOBIN YVON LabRAM HR Raman spectrometer) with a 514 nm Ar ion laser was used to take the Raman spectra. Dynamic light scattering analysis and Zeta potential measurement was done using HORIBA SZ-100 Nanoparticle Size and Zeta Analyzer. MTT viability assay was utilized to check the biocompatibility of the samples. It was performed on a TECAN Spark Cyto multi-mode plate reader. NIKON ECLIPSE Ti2 microscope was used to obtain fluorescence microscope images.

The dark brown solution was freeze dried to quantify CDs in powder form to take the FTIR spectra. For all other characterizations and application purposes, the diluted solution form of CDs was used. The structure, optical properties, biocompatibility, cell imaging, use as a FA sensor via PL quenching, and sensing of FA in real samples were investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility studies and cell imaging using CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
For the cell cytotoxicity test, Neuro 2a or N2a cells were seeded in a 96 well plate at a concentration of 50,000 cells/ml and cultured in DMEM (Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium) supplemented with 10 % FBS (Fetal bovine serum) and antibiotics at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 in the air for 24 h. Further, the cells were incubated with different concentrations of CDs for 48 h in triplicates. The cell viability was found through colorimetric MTT assays. The cells were washed with PBS (Phosphate buffered saline), then MTT reagent and medium were added to incubate for 4 h. The cells were lysed with 10 % SDS (Sodium dodecyl sulfate) and the absorbance at 570 nm was recorded by a TECAN microplate reader. Percentage cell viability was found using the equation,
(1)
%
c e l l
v i a b i l i t y =
Absorbance
o f
t h e
c e l l s
t r e a t e d
w i t h
C D s
Absorbance
o f
t h e
u n t r e a t e d
c e l l s
×
100

For cellular imaging, N2a cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10 % FBS, and cultured with 50 µg/mL CDs prepared for a growth time of 1 h (CD1) and 6 h (CD6) respectively at 37 °C. After incubation for 6 h, the cells were washed three times with pH 7.4 PBS to remove the existing media and the non-internalized nanoparticles, and was kept in PBS for optical imaging by a Nikon ECLIPSE Ti2 fluorescence microscope.

CDs solution was prepared by mixing 10 μL of the as-prepared solution in 3 mL of DI water (0.11 μg/mL). From that, 2 mL was taken into a cuvette with 1 mL DI water and that was considered as the reference. Different concentrations of FA were prepared by a method of dilution and 1 mL of FA solution was added to 2 mL of CDs solution for PL measurement. Both were mixed well in the cuvette itself and no incubation was done. The fluorescence spectra of CDs on the addition of different biomolecules were also studied using the same method. The change in fluorescence intensity of CDs and CD + FA mixture internalized in to N2a cells was measured quantitatively. For that N2a cells were seeded in black96 well plates and incubated for 24 h before treating the cells with 50 μg/ml CDs for 6 h. Stipulated concentrations of FA were added to the wells and fluorescencereading was taken immediately and after 4 h of incubation using TECAN Spark cyto multi-mode plate reader at emission 450 nm. Plasma sample was collected from a healthy Albino rat (SD Rat). FA was added to the diluted plasma sample (1 mL plasma in 10 mL DI water) and the procedure was repeated.

The morphology and size distribution of the synthesized nanoparticles were characterized by using TEM. TEM images of as-prepared CD1 and CD6 are shown in Fig. 2 (a) and Fig. 1S(c). The CDs have a spherical shape and narrow size distribution. The average diameter of the CD1 sample is 2.82 ± 0.65 nm and for the CD6 sample is 4.04 ± 1.1 nm (Fig. 1S(a) and (b)). Smaller particles were obtained for a small duration of hydrothermal synthesis. As the growth time increases, smaller particles agglomerate to form larger ones [28]. The lattice spacing of the CDs was calculated from the HRTEM images and was found to be 0.21 nm and 0.22 nm for CD1 and CD6 respectively. This indicates the presence of (100) facet graphitic core in the sample (Fig. 1S(d) and (e)) [6,25]. The amorphous nature arising from the surface functional groups of the carbon dots is evident in the SAED pattern (Fig. 1S(f) and (g)) [29].

Structural analysis was further done using XRD. XRD pattern of both the samples CD1 and CD6 shows a broad peak (Fig. 2S) at 24° corresponding to the (002) lattice plane of carbon based materials with lots of sp3 disorder [30]. The broadness of the peak indicates the amorphous nature of the CDs produced [16,31]. The XRD pattern showed no considerable change with the decrease in growth time in the hydrothermal method.

The role of surface functional groups is critical while considering the properties of CDs [32]. Functional groups on the surface of CDs were investigated using ATR-IR spectra (Fig. 3S(a)). The peak at 3254 cm−1 corresponds to the NH bond [33] and the broad peak around 3077 cm−1 and 2941 cm−1 show the stretching vibration of OH and CH bonds respectively [34,35]. The CC, CO and CO stretching bands of carboxylic ester groups are at 1643 cm−1 and 1288 cm−1 [36,37] and the vibrational absorption band of the amide III group or CN bond is present at 1381 cm−1 [23,38]. The ATR-IR results confirm the presence of –OH, –COOH and –NH2 functional groups on the prepared CDs [37,39]. Moreover, the observation of NH and CN bonds is evidence of the effective doping of N atoms on the CDs prepared. Thus, the precursor EDA gives appreciable N doping to the CDs prepared in our study.

The extent of distortion and structural defects in CDs can be find out using Raman analysis. Fig. 3S(b) shows the characteristic Raman spectrum of the sample CD1. Raman signals for CDs were not evident due to their high luminescence background under our measurement conditions. But the drop cast solution of CDs on silver nanoparticles shows the D band at 1333 cm−1 and the G band at 1580 cm−1 due to the surface-enhanced Raman scattering [40]. Here the D band corresponds to the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination lane of disordered graphite and the G band corresponds to the vibrations of sp2 carbon atoms in a two dimensional hexagonal lattice [30]. The intensity ratio ID/IG, which represents the extent of distortion and the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon was 1.48 indicating the presence of structural defects in the CDs [30], confirming the formation of amorphous carbon dots instead of graphene quantum dots [36]. These surface defects are associated with the nitrogenated and oxygenated groups on the surface of CDs [41]. The observed Raman spectra for the CD1 sample were found to be similar to that of the sample CD6 (Fig. 3S(c)), suggesting identical distortion and structural defects in both.

The optical properties of CDs such as absorption, excitation and emission need to be studied in detail to use it for luminescence-based applications. Fig. 3S(d) shows the absorption, excitation and emission spectra of CD1. The shoulder absorption peak found at approximately 238 nm in the absorption spectra corresponds to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains (CC) which requires very large energy for transition. The strong absorption peak at approximately 348 nm corresponds to the n-π* transition of the CO or CN group and it requires less energy compared to the π-π* transition of CC, revealing the amorphous nature of CDs [1–3]. The excitation spectra have a peak at 360 nm, which is slightly shifted from the absorption peak and this absence of correlation between absorption and excitation peak can be explained by anti-Vavilov’s rule. Vavilov’s rule explains that, the QY is independent of the excitation wavelength used for measurement [42].

The diluted CD solution, which is transparent and light yellow in color under normal light, shows bright blue luminescence under UV excitation (Fig. 1). At an excitation of 360 nm an emission peak was observed at 450 nm (Fig. 2(b)). QY for the samples was found by measuring the total light used for excitation and emission using an integrating sphere. For both CD1 and CD6, the average QY was 30 % for 6 repeated measurements. The high QY of CDs can be correlated to the content of nitrogen doping [13]. The CDs show an excitation dependent emission and the emission peak exhibits a slight shift toward longer wavelengths especially as the excitation wavelength increased from 360 nm to 430 nm (Fig. 2(b) for CD1 and Fig. 4S for CD6) indicating a non-uniform size as observed in TEM and the presence of surface states in the prepared CDs [5]. An emission peak at 450 nm suggests that the emission is from the surface domains and not from the carbon- core [43]. This also indicates the presence of more than one luminescence center on CDs and that the emission is not originating from the molecular fluorophores [32]. The PL spectra further confirm the non-uniform size observed in the TEM analysis because of the excitation dependent PL emission with a broad full width at half maximum (FWHM)[13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and cell imaging using CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Water-soluble fluorescent CDs with minimal cytotoxicity are ideal for cell imaging applications [6,8]. Herein, the cytotoxicity of the CD1 and CD6 was evaluated by MTT viability assay using N2a or neuro 2a cells. Neuro 2a is the cancer cell line derived from the nervous tissue of mice [44,45]. The result in Fig. 2(c) shows that these CDs don’t exhibit notable cytotoxicity even at a higher concentration of 500 μg/mL and CD1 has a slightly better percentage of cell viability compared to CD6 for an incubation period of 48 h. The obtained high cell viability makes the prepared CDs a potential candidate for in vitro cell imaging [46].

Fig. 3 shows the fluorescence microscope images of N2a cells after treatment with 50 μg/mL CD1 and CD6 after an incubation period of 6 h using the blue microscopic filter having the specifications, Ex 361–389, DM 415 and BA 430–490. It is clear that both the samples entered into the cytoplasm of the cells and displays bright blue luminescence. The structural, optical and cell viability analysis of the prepared CD indicates that the CD1, prepared by one hour of hydrothermal synthesis in the present work is as good as CD6, prepared by six hours of hydrothermal synthesis, making CD1 the optimized sample. Thus the present work comes with an efficient CD prepared at a lower synthesis time, compared with the works reported to date.

On the incremental addition of FA to the CD solution, quenching in the photoluminescence intensity was observed. Quantification of the change in luminescence intensity, determines the quantity of FA added. A good analytical sensor requires three important qualities: selectivity, stability and sensitivity. Sensing studies were done as per the ICH guidelines [47].
a) Selectivity

The selectivity experiment was carried out to check whether our system is specific for the recognition of FA. For that 1 mL of 1 mM solutions of various biological interfering substances was added to 2 mL of CD solution and the photoluminescence emission intensity was measured. Small biomolecules such as Histidine, Tryptophan, Tyrosine, vitamin C, Dopamine, Catechol, FA and saccharide such as glucose were chosen as the interfering compounds. The compounds selected are those which usually interfere with actual FA sensing in blood samples. A considerable change in the emission intensity of CDs was observed only with the addition of FA solution (Fig. 5S(a)). The emission intensity remains almost unchanged for all other interfering substances, indicating that the prepared CDs show high selectivity towards FA. The effect of interference of these biomolecules in the presence of FA was tested and observed negligible changes in the emission intensity (Fig. 5S(b). Hence, CDs can be used in complex samples, to measure FA with high selectivity.
b) Stability

The stability of the probe was tested by observing the changes in PL intensity at different pH and storage times. The integral intensity of emission was found to be nearly constant in the pH range 3–10 (Fig. 5S(c)) and for a very long duration of time approximately 1 year (Fig. 5S(d)). The PL stability under a wide range of pH for a very long period makes it beneficial for imaging and sensing studies. The dependence of PL intensity on the concentration of CDs was tested and the optimum concentration was obtained as 10 μL of as prepared CD solution in 3 mL of DI water (Fig. 5S(e)) and this particular concentration was fixed for further analysis. The PL emission intensity of the CDs in each batch of synthesis was observed to be constant (Fig. 5S(f)) confirming the stability in each batch. The influence of incubation time on the PL intensity of the CD + FA mixture was evaluated and found that unlike the previous reports (Table 1S), the PL intensity hardly changed with incubation time up to 90 min indicating faster kinetics (Fig. 5S(g)) [20]. This helps in the fast detection of FA using the CDs.
c) Sensitivity

Since fluorescence quenching of CDs was observed upon incremental addition of FA (Fig. 6S(a) and (b)), sensitivity can be quantitatively characterized by the calibration curve of fluorescence quenching (F0/F) against various concentrations of FA which is known as the Stern-Volmer (S-V) plot (Fig. 4 (a)). Here F0 and F are the integral intensity of emission of CDs in the absence and presence of FA at 360 nm excitation respectively. The calculation of limit of detection (LoD) was carried out using the formula;
(2)
LoD = 3.3
σ S
where σ is the standard deviation of response and S is the slope of the calibration curve obtained from the regression analysis [48]. LoD was found to be 6 nM and a good linear relationship was attained in the range of 1–50 nM. The suitability of the prepared CDs as a FA sensor was checked in this particular range as the level of FA in human blood samples is in this range.

The linear variation of intensity ratio with the concentration of FA with a correlation coefficient of 0.98 proves that CDs can be used as an FA sensor. A linear S-V plot indicates the occurrence of only one type of quenching and a single class of fluorophore having equal accessibility of all to the quencher [49]. The value of LoD obtained and the range of linear behavior is much smaller compared to the earlier works of CDs using the same precursors [26,50]. This reduction in the limit of detection can be attributed to the selective surface functional groups like –NH2 on the synthesized CDs. The selectivity and sensitivity of CDs are highly dependent on the precursors and synthesis conditions, since surface functionalization groups are determined by this [51]. A comparison showing the precursors and LoD of previously reported works on CDs based FA sensors, with this work is given in Table 1S. From the table, it is clear that the hydrothermal duration of synthesis will be influencing the surface states of CDs and this may be the reason behind the lower LoD obtained in the present study.
d) Quenching mechanism

The quenching of fluorescence can be due to a variety of molecular interactions such as static quenching, collisional/ dynamic quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) etc. The collisional quenching is due to the collision between fluorophore and quencher and the static quenching is due to the formation of a non-fluorescent complex between them [49,52]. Fig. 4(b) gives the absorbance spectra of FA and the emission and excitation spectra of CD1. The lack of overlap between the emission spectra of CDs and the absorption spectra of FA rules out the possibility of FRET and the overlap between the excitation spectra of CDs and the absorbance spectra of FA indicates the chances for inner filter effect (IFE) [26]. Fig. 7S represents the TEM image of the CD + FA mixture, in which CDs are found to be attached to FA.

Fluorescence lifetime, absorption spectra and Raman analysis are techniques to distinguish between static and dynamic quenching. The lifetime decay curves of CDs and CD + FA mixtures coincide as in Fig. 8S and from Table 1 , the lifetime τ of CDs and the CD + FA mixture was found to be almost constant. The unaffected τ value indicates the quenching is static in nature due to the formation of a non-fluorescent complex between CDs and FA. If the complex formed was fluorescent, it would have contributed to the lifetime measurements, changing the τ value [49,52]. Long fluorescence lifetime compared to other reported values can be assigned due to the rich long-chain surface functional groups which could provide a better trapping effect and increase the sensitivity [32].

Zeta potential of CDs was found to be −42.7 mV which was increased to −56.2 mV on the addition of FA. A zeta potential magnitude of greater than 30 mV implies that the nanoparticles are stable in aqueous form without any chances of aggregation/deposition [53]. On the addition of FA the presence of negatively charged functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl or carbonyl groups increased on the surface of CDs leading to the formation of a more stable complex [54].

In the absorption spectra of CDs with different concentrations of FA (Fig. 5 (a)), the peak corresponding to FA is visible and there is a slight shift in the carbon dots absorption peak when FA is added.
This perturbation in UV–vis absorption spectra further confirms the static quenching [49,55,56].

Fig. 5(b) shows that the average hydrodynamic diameter of CDs is around 10.6 nm. But with the addition of 50 nM FA solution, the diameter increased to the micrometer range and no peak was visible in the nanometer region indicating the complex formation (Fig. 5(c)) [57]. The hydrodynamic diameter obtained for the CD + FA mixture matches that of the size observed from the SEM image of FA (Fig. 9S), supporting the formation of a complex. The quenching of luminescence by the complex formation between CDs and FA can be illustrated schematically as in Fig. 1.

The Raman spectra were taken for the CD + FA mixture (1 and 50 μM FA) (Fig. 5(d)). The peaks of FA were visible in the CD + FA (50 μM) mixture with a shift in wavenumber to shorter values. The shift in wavenumber to lower or higher values depends on the modification in the structure related to the vibrational mode of the peak confirming the complex formation further [58]. A blue shift was observed in the D and G bands, suggesting increased compressive stress and reduction in bond length and lattice parameters of CDs on the addition of FA [59,60]. An increase in ID/IG ratio from 1.48 to 2.50 indicates an increment in surface functional groups, mainly the attachment of aliphatic moieties and hydrocarbons to the graphitic structure. Increase in D band also indicates an increase in oxygen containing groups in the surface of CDs which are contributed by FA [54].

The FTIR vibrational peaks of CD + FA mixture does not show any new peak compared to CDs indicating the absence of a covalent bond formation between CDs and FA (Fig. 5(e)). But the blue shift of CN (1384 cm−1 to 1394 cm−1) and NN bonds (1100 cm−1 to 1109 cm−1) [61] on the addition of FA suggests that the electron distribution in the molecular bond has changed causing a reduction in bond length due to the attachment of FA to CDs [60]. A reduction in intensity of NH bending vibration at 1320 cm−1 suggests the attachment of FA is occurring through N in the carbon dot and broadening of –OH and –NH peak around 3400 cm−1 on the addition of FA indicates that the binding is by intermolecular hydrogen bonding [62,63].
e) Determination of FA in real samples

The practicability of the method was evaluated by the analysis of FA in a complex matrix like blood plasma of albino rats (SD rats). Blood from the rats was collected in EDTA vials and centrifuged at 3000 rpm to separate out the plasma and was diluted with deionized water in the ratio of 1:10. Different amounts of FA were spiked into the plasma solution and the experimental method chosen was standard addition [30,64,65]. The standard addition method goes by the doctrine, that ‘the complexity of matching the matrix of the standards to that of the sample can be avoided by conducting standardization in the sample’ [66]. Table 2 shows the added amount and the found amount of FA with a recovery percentage lying between 81 and 114 % and the percentage relative standard deviation (%RSD) for n = 6 was no more than 10.4 %, which indicates that our assay is capable of the analysis of FA in real samples [26]. The found amount was validated using an existing laboratory technique electrochemiluminescence immunoassay (ECLIA) and obtained matching results with an accuracy of more than 60 %. As the maximum limit of detection of FA using ECLIA is 45 nM, accurate values for added FA concentration of more than 45 nM cannot be obtained with this technique.

The decrease in percentage fluorescence intensity of CDs and CD + FA mixture, internalized in N2a cells, with increase in FA concentration was observed (Fig. 10S) clearly when measured quantitatively using the TECAN Spark cyto-multimode plate reader. 4 h of incubation with FA enhances the quenching compared to the results obtained without incubation. Cell may take some time for internalization of FA which eventually quenches the emission from CDs. This result along with FA sensing in plasma samples makes the CDs a better probe for the sensing of FA in biomedical field.

Water soluble, nitrogen doped, and blue luminescent CDs were synthesized through a cost-effective, single-step hydrothermal method using CA and EDA. The growth time of hydrothermal synthesis was optimized to 1 h. Good biocompatibility was observed for the CDs by MTT cell viability assay, and it was effectively used for N2a cell imaging. Diluted CDs solution acts as a fast luminescence-based detector of FA with a limit of detection of 6 nM. Static quenching of photoluminescence due to the formation of a complex between CDs and FA explains the detection mechanism. The prepared CD probe as a FA sensor was used to detect FA in spiked plasma samples of albino mice. The results validated using the clinical method ECLIA, gave more than 60 % accuracy, suggesting the use of the developed probe for practical studies in the medical field.
This work was funded by Kerala State Council for Science, Technology and Environment (KSCSTE) under the KSCSTE Research fellowship scheme (KSCSTE/687/2019 -FSHP -PS).

T.K. Krishnapriya: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. S Prasanth: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Ayswaria Deepti: Methodology, Investigation, Validation. P.S. Baby Chakrapani: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. A.S. Asha: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing. M.K. Jayaraj: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by Kerala State Council for Science, Technology and Environment (KSCSTE) under the KSCSTE Research fellowship scheme (KSCSTE/687/2019-FSHP-PS). The authors acknowledge DST FIST for the FESEM facility at the Department of Physics, CUSAT, STIC-CUSAT for TEM measurement, the International School of Photonics, CUSAT for DLS measurement, and Dr. Saritha G Bhatt, Department of Biotechnology, CUSAT for fruitful discussions.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Static and dynamic fluorescence quenching experiments for the physical chemistry laboratory
